coredata.dc:title: Non-conjugated polymer dots for fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode detection of quercetin
coredata.dc:creator: Fan, Yu
Yao, Jie
Huang, Mengke
Linghu, Chenxi
Guo, Jinlin
Li, Yang
coredata.dc:description: Due to the biochemical and pharmacological activities, the convenient and effective detection of quercetin (Qc) is very important for biochemistry, pharmaceutical chemistry and clinical medicine. A kind of non-conjugated polymer dots (NCPDs) was used as a versatile and sensitive dual-mode optical output for Qc detection, which was synthesized by hyperbranched poly(ethylenimine) (PEI) and l-threonine via environmentallyfriendly way. The dual-mode method proposed in this work had high sensitivity and definiteselectivity for Qc detection. Additionally, it was convenient for the naked eyes to observe the fluorescence brightness and color change.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Non-conjugated polymer dots
Quercetin detection
Dual-mode
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Assay of Qc in real samples
Detection of Qc by the dual-mode assay
Plausible quenching mechanism for the as-prepared NCPDs with Qc
Recovery tests in red wine samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (A) NCPDs: scale bar 50 nm, (B) NCPDs: scale bar 5 nm, (C) CDs: scale bar 20 nm, (D) CDs: scale bar 5 nm. (E) Hydrodynamic particle size of NCPDs measured by DLS.

XPS spectra of NCPDs (A) C 1 s region, (B) N 1 s region, XPS spectra of CDs (C) C 1 s region, (D) N 1 s region. (E) FTIR spectrum of the threonine (a), PEI (b) and NCPDs (c). (F) UV–vis absorption spectrum of threonine (red), PEI (dark brown) and NCPDs (blue); Inset: the photograph of the NCPDs under the visible light (left) and UV light of 365 nm (right). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of NCPDs. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of NCPDs under different excitations from 310 to 380 nm with a 5 nm increment. The influences of UV irradiation time (C) and NaCl concentration (D) on the fluorescence intensity of NCPDs; (E) Scavenging effect of NCPDs on ABTS radicals.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of NCPDs versus various concentrations of Qc (0–65 μg/mL); (B) Fluorescence intensity changes of NCPDs in the presence of increasing concentration of Qc, insets: linear curve of F0/F vs concentration of Qc from 0.05 to 10 μg/mL and from 12.5 to 30 μg/mL, where F0 and F denote the fluorescence intensity of NCPDs before and after addition of Qc, respectively. (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of NCPDs versus various concentrations of Qc (0–27.5 μg/mL); (D) Linear curve of absorbance vs concentration of Qc ranging from 0.5 μg/mL to 27.5 μg/mL. Corresponding photographs of the solutions under UV light of 365 nm (E) and visible light (F).

(A) Fluorescence lifetime of NCPDs in the absence and in the presence of Qc; (B) UV–vis absorption spectrum of Qc, NCPDs and NCPDs-Qc solution; (C) UV–vis absorption of Qc and the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of NCPDs.
(A) Illustration of synthesis process of NCPDs and the detection of Qc. (B) Photograph of the precursor solution, NCPDs and NCPDs-Qc under the visible light (top) and UV light of 365 nm (down).

head.ce:title: Non-conjugated polymer dots for fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode detection of quercetin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Medical Technology, Chengdu University of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Chengdu 611137, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chengdu University of Traditional Chinese Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Medical Technology, Chengdu University of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Chengdu 611137, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmacy, Key Laboratory of Standardization of Chinese Medicine, Ministry of Education Chengdu University of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Chengdu 611137, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Standardization of Chinese Medicine
Ministry of Education Chengdu University of Traditional Chinese Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmacy, Key Laboratory of Standardization of Chinese Medicine, Ministry of Education Chengdu University of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Chengdu 611137, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to the biochemical and pharmacological activities, the convenient and effective detection of quercetin (Qc) is very important for biochemistry, pharmaceutical chemistry and clinical medicine. A kind of non-conjugated polymer dots (NCPDs) was used as a versatile and sensitive dual-mode optical output for Qc detection, which was synthesized by hyperbranched poly(ethylenimine) (PEI) and l-threonine via environmentallyfriendly way. The dual-mode method proposed in this work had high sensitivity and definiteselectivity for Qc detection. Additionally, it was convenient for the naked eyes to observe the fluorescence brightness and color change.

ce:section.ce:para: Quercetin (3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-4Hchromen-4-one, Qc), a natural flavonoid present in most plants, fruits and vegetables, has unique biological properties including antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, anti-proliferative, antiviral, antibacterial and cardiovascular protection effect (Cai, Fang, Dou, Yu, & Zhai, 2013). As documented, Qc can inhibit the proliferation of different types of cancer cells by regulating cellular processes and inhibiting the growth of cancer cells (Wang et al., 2016). Considering this fact, the detection or determination of Qc is very important for biochemistry, pharmaceutical chemistry and clinical medicine due to the biochemical and pharmacological activities. To date, numerous analytical techniques have been applied to the quantification of Qc, including high performance liquid chromatography (Jafari & Hadjmohammadi, 2020), liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (Kumar, Verma, Singh, & Viswanathan, 2015), electrochemistry (Stefanov, Negut, Gugoasa, & Staden, 2020) and UV–vis spectroscopy (Soylak, Ozdemir, & Yilmaz, 2020). However, quercetin has low water solubility with a solubility of only 0.01 mg/mL in water (Gao et al., 2011), making it difficult to combine high levels of quercetin directly into a water-based matrix.

Due to its high sensitivity, good selectivity and operational simplicity, fluorescence technology has come into the focus of utilization in Qc determination. Pardo-Barrela et al. utilized the quenching of Qc towards the inherent fluorescence tryptophan residues of BSA to achieve the quantitative determination of Qc, and compared the method with HPLC (Pardo-Barrela, Lago-Crespo, Lage-Yusty, & López-Hernández, 2015). It revealed that fluorescence spectroscopy could be considered as a rapid alternative analysis method for Qc analysis, and there was a statistically significant relationship between the data obtained by the two methods. Pavun et al. developed a simple method for Qc based on the fluorescence properties of the aluminum (III)-quercetin complex (Pavun et al., 2014). Similarly, a fluorometric method for Qc detection was described based on the formation of highly fluorescent tatraphenyldiboroxide-Qc by Gugler et al. (Gugler & Dengler, 1973). K. Alabri et al. realized the detection of quercetin by a fluorescence spectroscopy-partial least square regression method (Alabri et al., 2018). At present, a number of fluorophores are applied in Qc sensing, such as ZnS quantum dots (Wu & Chen, 2014), carbon dots (Zou et al., 2015), AuNCs (Chen, Qian, Chen, & Chen, 2012), g-C3N4 (Xu, Chen, & Ma, 2018) and CdSe/ZnS quantum dot (Dwiecki et al., 2015). However, the development of these common fluorescent probes has been limited due to their disadvantages such as high toxicity, complex process, poor water solubility and poor photostability (Song et al., 2019).

Research on non-conjugated polymer dots (NCPDs) which possess aggregated non-conjugated polymer structures, remains in continuous focus due to their application potential (Zhu, Song, Shao, Zhao, & Yang, 2015; Tao, Zhu, Feng, Zheng, & Yang, 2020). They have attracted a great deal of interest in fluorescence imaging, drug delivery and gene transfection, because NCPDs are endowed with some inherent merits such as excellent luminous performance, good stability and good biocompatibility (Li et al., 2014). The PL centers of NCPDs are rarely discussed because they do not have typical fluorophores. The crosslink-enhanced emission (CEE) effect is reported to explain the luminescence mechanism of NCPDs containing aliphatic tertiary amines or heteroatom-containing double bonds (CO, CN, NO), which traditionally are not regarded as fluorescent chromophores (Zhu, Song, Shao, Zhao, & Yang, 2015; Zhu et al., 2014). Zhu et al. used branched polyethyleneimine (PEI) as a model non-conjugated polymer to illustrate the CEE principle that the enhanced PL originated from the decreased vibration and rotation of crosslinked PEI (Zhu et al., 2014). Fei et al. observed that the PLQY increased with the degree of crosslinking hyperbranched PEI by divinylsulfone through Michael addition (Fei, Yang, Shao, Wan, & Xin, 2010). Therefore, some of the emission structures formed during the crosslinking process are also important for enhancing PL. The inherent virtues of NCPDs with special structures and ample terminal groups drive the dreams of exploring their possible applications in quercetin detection.

Herein, a simple synthesis of NCPDs with eco-friendly solvents was achieved using PEI and l-threonine as raw material. The NCPDs exhibited excellent optical properties and favorable stability. Then, a dual-mode assay of fluorescence and colorimetric was constructed based on one system (NCPDs-Qc) for Qc determination. Compared with the single mode assays of one output signal, the dual-mode strategy could provide a simpler, more effective and more reliable way (Liang et al., 2021; Zhu et al., 2021). Scheme 1 was the illustration of synthesis process of NCPDs and the detection of Qc. In the presence of Qc, the fluorescence intensity of NCPDs increased and the absorbance decreased. The details on the preparation and characterization of the NCPDs, response performances of the NCPDs-Qc system, and possible mechanism are following.

Hyperbranched poly(ethylenimine) (PEI, H(NHCH2CH2)nNH2, Mw = 25 000 Da), l-threonine, [2,2′-azinobis-(3-ethyl-benzothiazolin-6-sulfonic acid)] diammonium salt (ABTS) were received from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Quercetin (Qc), glucose and urea were obtained from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). From Chengdu Glip Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Sichuan, China), ginsenoside Rb1, ginsenoside Rg1, ginsenoside Rd, notoginsenoside R1, notoginsenoside R2, aconitine, hypaconitine and mesaconitine were purchased. Other chemical reagents were analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.25 MΩ was employed throughout the experiments.

The fluorescence spectra were recorded by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-4600, Hitachi, Japan). The fluorescence lifetimes and absolute quantum yields were measured by using FLS 1000 stable transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was conducted using Nicolet IS10 (USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was tested on Thermo escalab 250Xi. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on FEI Tecnai G2 F30 (USA). The dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta potentials were carried out on Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Thermo, America). All images were captured by a smartphone camera (iPhone 7).

The non-conjugated polymer dots (NCPDs) were prepared with PEI and l-threonine as raw material through a facile hydrothermal method (Scheme 1A). Briefly, 0.05 g branched PEI (Mw = 25 000 Da) was dissolved in 15 mL 0.1 M pH 7.0 PBS. After stirring uniformly, 0.3 g l-threonine was quickly added to the solution. Then, the precursor solution was sealed in a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave and heated at 150 ℃ for 6 h. After cooling to the room temperature, the yellowish solution was purified through a dialysis bag (3 500 Da, molecular weight cutoff) for two days. The final products were acquired by freeze-drying and kept at 4 ℃ until further use and characterization. It is worthwhile pointing out that, the resultant non-conjugated polymer dots exhibited intense cyan fluorescence under 365 nm UV irradiation compared to the weak luminescence of the precursor solution (Scheme 1B). For comparison, pure carbon dots (CDs) were obtained under the same preparation conditions (except without adding PEI).

1.99 mL of the NCPDs and 10 μL of Qc with various concentrations were added in the quartz cell (1 × 1 cm), and then fluorescence spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra of NCPDs in the absence and presence of QC were recorded. For quantitative analysis of quercetin, fluorescence measurements were carried out with the parameters set as: excitation wavelength (λEx) at 340 nm, emission wavelength (λEm) range of 360–650 nm, scan speed 240 nm/min, PMT voltage 700 V, response 0.1 s and slit widths = 5 nm, 5 nm.

[2,2′-azinobis-(3-ethyl-benzothiazolin-6-sulfonic acid)] diammonium salt (ABTS) assay was tested according to the reported method with minor modification (Wang, Li, Bao, Gao, & Tao, 2018). ABTS radical solution (ABTS∙+) was obtained by 7 mM ABTS and 4.95 mM K2S2O8 reacting at 4 ℃ inthedark for 12 h. The solution was diluted 5 mM PBS until the absorbance between 0.7 and 0.8 at 730 nm. Next, the diluted ABTS radical solution (1.99 mL) was mixed with 10 μL various concentrations NCPDs for 10 min to depolarize. Finally, the absorbance of mixture was collected at 730 nm. Ascorbic acid (AA) was used as a control. The percentage of ABTS scavenging activity of the NCPDs was calculated by the following formula:
A B T S
s c a v e n g i n g
a c t i v i t y
%
=
( 1 -
A 1
-
A 2
A 0
)
× 100
A0 and A1 are the absorbance of mixture in the absence and presence of NCPDs, respectively. A2 is the absorbance of the sample under the same conditions as A1 with PBS replacing ABTS∙+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of Qc in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Two different brands of red wine purchased from local supermarket were chosen as the samples. Before analysis, the red wine was centrifuged at 15000 rpm for 10 min and filtered with a 0.45 μm membrane filter to remove any undesirable impurities. The recovery experiment was carried out by the standard addition method to study the applicability of the analytical assay. Various concentrations of Qc were added to the red wine, and Qc was determined through the above-mentioned operation process.
The Origin 2019b software and Microsoft Excel 2019 were used for simple regression analysis and variance (ANOVA) of data obtained by UV–Vis and fluorescence. The level of significance was p < 0.05.

The particle size of NCPDs was measured by TEM and DLS. The NCPDs exhibited irregular spherical shape and size about 20–30 nm by TEM (Fig. 1 A and 1B). Moreover, the edges of NCPDs were not smooth and the center was darker,which was attributed to the presence of PEI chains enclosing the carbon nucleus with threonine as the nucleus. Only a few lattice fringes occurred (Fig. 1B), possibly due to further slightly carbonization or the crystallization of the polymer (Tao, Song, Zhu, Shao, & Yang, 2017). Compared with the NCPDs, the CDs (without PEI involved in the synthesis) were smaller in size about 5–15 nm, and the edges of CDs looked more rounded (Fig. 1C and 1D). The existence of these PEI chains could give the NCPDs long-term stability in aqueous solution and excellent optical performance. Fig. 1E was DLS data and revealed that the average hydrodynamic size of NCPDs in water was 98 nm, which was larger than TEM measurements. The possible reason for this discrepancy was that DLS determined the diameter of NCPDs in the solution which in a “swollen” state, while TEM measured the diameter of NCPDs after drying (Yu et al., 2012).

The XPS results of NCPDs and CDs are shown in Fig. 2 A-B and Fig. 2C-D, respectively. For NCPDs: the C 1 s peak was deconvoluted into four contributions (Fig. 2A) at 284.8 eV (CC), 285.8 eV (CN), 287.4 eV (CN) and 288.7 eV (CO); the N 1 s peak (Fig. 2B) was the sum of 398.6 eV (CN), 399.7 eV (C-NH2) and 400.8 eV (NH). For CDs: the C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2C) exhibited three peaks at 284.8 eV (CC), 286.2 eV (CO) and 288.1 eV (OC-O); the N 1 s peak (Fig. 2D) consisted of two components at 399.7 eV (C-NH2) and 401.4 eV (NO). The biggest difference between the NCPDs and CDs was that the NCPDs formed a CN bond and the percentage of amino groups increased from the bare threonine to crosslinked NCPDs. Hyperbranched PEI, a dendritic polymer, contains abundant amino groups. Under the high temperature and pressure, the hydroxyl of threonine was easily transformed to carbonyl group via oxidation (Liu et al., 2016), and then the carbonyl group was able to react with the amino group of PEI to form a CN bond by Schiff base reaction.

The FTIR results of NCPDs fit well with the presence of the above-described structural features. Fig. 2E showed the FTIR spectra of threonine, PEI and NCPDs. The absorption peak centered at 3425 cm−1 belonged to the overlap absorption of NH and OH stretching vibration, while the CH stretching vibration was situated at 2966 and 2839 cm−1. The absorption peak around 1628 cm−1 was assigned to CN bond (consistent with Liu’s work) (Liu et al., 2016), confirming the formation of Schiff base bonds and NCPDs copolymer. The absorption peak at 1126 cm−1 indicated the asymmetric stretching vibrations of CN. The zeta potential of NCPDs was + 6.36 mv, which was much lower than that of the PEI (reported as + 50 mv (Lee et al., 2011)), indicating the amino groups of the PEI in the NCPDs were partly reacted with the threonine. The appearance of multiple functional groups of carbon and nitrogen elements suggested the successful synthesis of non-conjugated polymer dots.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of PEI (dark brown), threonine (red) and NCPDs (blue) were recorded and shown in Fig. 2F. Compared with PEI and threonine, two new absorption peaks appeared at around 280 nm and 364 nm for NCPDs, which ascribed to π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains from the carbon core and the n-π* transition of the surface moieties with nitrogen atoms (Du et al., 2020). This phenomenon further confirmed that PEI reacted with threonine to form the CN bond. The illustration in Fig. 2F showed that the NCPDs solution was yellowish under natural light and cyan under UV light (365 nm).

In conventional fluorescent materials, CC bond or CC bond or carbon-based π-electrons are considered to be the main building units of PL centers. However, some other chemical groups with weak fluorescence (we refer to these potential PL groups as sub-fluorophores), such as heteroatomic double bonds (CO, CN, NO) and single bonds (amino-based group, CO), can also show increased PL with appropriate fixation (Zhu, Song, Shao, Zhao, & Yang, 2015; Vallan et al., 2018). Zhu et al. investigated that the center of PL was the amino-based group molecular state (mainly secondary and tertiary amines) in the PEI system, but the radiation recombination was limited (Zhu et al., 2014). In this work, the PEI chains were crosslinked and immobilized on the carbon core via a hydrothermal method. CN bond was produced during the synthesis through the Schiff base reaction, which might contribute to the formation of compact and rigid polymer structure and thus assisted to suppress nonradiative pathway and facilitate radiative relaxation (Liu et al., 2016). Covalently-bond crosslink-enhanced emission (CEE) effect amplified the underlying PL properties of the sub-fluorophores group, leading to the PL enhancement (Tao, Zhu, Feng, Zheng, & Yang, 2020; Vallan, Urriolabeitia, Benito, & Maser, 2019).

NCPDs exhibited exceptionally bright cyan-fluorescence under UV light (365 nm), while PEI or threonine or precursor solution had no obvious fluorescence. In the fluorescence spectra (Fig. 3 A), NCPDs possessed optimal excitation and emission wavelengths at 340 nm and 470 nm with a stoke shift of 130 nm. The fluorescence emission spectra of the NCPDs at different excitation wavelengths are shown in Fig. 3B. Excitation-dependent PL behaviors were observed. When the excitation wavelength of the NCPDs increased from 310 to 380 nm, the fluorescence maximum first increased and then decreased, accompanied by a red shift. In addition, the absolute quantum yield of the NCPDs was measured to be 0.057. The calculated average fluorescence lifetime was 4.25 ns for NCPDs, with two components, 1.6917 ns (~18.18%) and 4.8173 ns (~81.82%). The fast lifetimes indicate that the radiative relaxation occurs by means of fluorescence.

As shown in Scheme 1B, the images of precursor solution, NCPDs and NCPDs-Qc under ultraviolet and visible light were compared. Colorless and transparent precursor solution showed little fluorescence under ultraviolet light. The yellowish NCPDs showed intense cyan fluorescence under 365 nm UV irradiation. After adding quercetin, the cyan fluorescence disappeared and the color of the solution became more yellow. As can be seen from Fig. S1, the mixture of precursor solution-Qc and water-Qc were turbid, indicating a low solubility between them.

The influences of UV irradiation time and NaCl concentration on the fluorescence intensity of NCPDs were studied. As shown in Fig. 3C, with the increase of UV irradiation time, the fluorescence intensity of NCPDs had no significant change, signifying a charming photostability. Similar result was obtained in the influences of NaCl concentration (Fig. 3D). The unchanged fluorescence emission of NCPDs was observed in the NaCl concentration from 0 to 4.0 mol/L. In addition, the solution of NCPDs were observed to be particularly stable for several months without any aggregation or precipitation. These results indicated that the NCPDs could be developed as a fluorescent probe.

ABTS free radicals have been widely used to detect the primary free radical scavenging capacity of plant extracts or antioxidants. The ABTS∙+ radical forms from the reaction ABTS-e-→ABTS∙+ and reacts quickly with samples to make the mixed solution fade. The obvious feature is a decrease in absorbance. The more obvious the fading reaction, the higher total antioxidant capacity of the samples is (Xie, Hu, Wang, & Zeng, 2014). As displayed in Fig. 3E, the ABTS free radical assay was dependent on NCPDs concentration from 0 to 300 μg/mL. The scavenging activity increased from 14 to 88% with the concentration of NCPDs ranged from 0 to 300 μg/mL. The half inhibition effective concentration of NCPDs was about 150 μg/mL, which was much higher than that of ascorbic acid (2.5 μg/mL) (Du et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Qc by the dual-mode assay
ce:section.ce:para: A sensitive dual-mode detection method was developed for Qc by fluorescence and colorimetric analysis. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence of the NCPDs was sensitive to Qc and gradually decreased with an increase in the Qc concentration (0.05–65 μg/mL), suggesting the performance of NCPDs to the detection of Qc. The relationship between the fluorescence intensity of NCPDs and Qc concentration was exhibited in the Fig. 4B, which was not linear.It is a fact that the fluorescence quenching data generally follow the Stern-Volmer formula, F0/F – 1 = K[Q], where F0 and F denote fluorescence intensity of the NCPDs before and after the introduction of Qc, K is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, and [Q] represents the concentration of Qc. As seen from the illustration in Fig. 4B, F0/F was linear with the concentration of quercetin in two segments, one was 0.05–10 μg/mL (R2 = 0.995) and the other was 12.5–30 μg/mL (R2 = 0.992). The detection limit was calculated to be 8.5 ng/mL according to three times standard deviation rule, indicating high sensitivity.

Meanwhile, the colorimetric method was also utilized to detect Qc. The absorption spectra of NCPDs in the presence of various concentration of Qc were shown in Fig. 4C. As the Qc concentration increased from 0.5 μg/mL to 27.5 μg/mL, the absorbance increased and the color of the solution became deeper. A good linear curve with R2 = 0.997 was obtained by linear fitting the absorbance versus the Qc concentration (Fig. 4D). In addition, the detection limit was 0.22 μg/mL (S/N = 3). The results demonstrated the feasibility of Qc sensing by using NCPDs system. Compared with other detection methods (Table S1), this work showed the advantages of dual-mode reporting, which could be mutually verified, making the detection more reliable. Moreover, the change of the colorimetric signal could be easily observed, so as to realize a visual inspection of Qc.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plausible quenching mechanism for the as-prepared NCPDs with Qc
ce:section.ce:para: Conventional quenching mechanisms including static quenching, dynamic quenching, Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), photo-induced electron transfer (PET) and inner filter effect (IFE) have been investigated extensively in recent literature (Zu et al., 2017). Static quenching occurs with the formation of non-fluorescent ground state complex through the interaction between the luminophore and quencher. In the dynamic quenching, the excited state molecules of luminophore returns to the ground state through the collision between the luminophore and quencher due to energy transfer or charge transfer. The FRET occurs when the emission spectrum of the donor overlap the absorption spectrum of the acceptor while the IFE requires a certain degree of spectral overlap between the absorption spectrum of the quencher and the excitation spectra of luminophore. The PET quenching mechanism is usually accompanied by a change in the fluorescence lifetime of the luminophore (Zu et al., 2017; Wu, Liu, Ge, Zhang, & Wang, 2011).

To understand the possible fluorescence quenching mechanism between NCPDs and Qc, fluorescence lifetimes, UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of NCPDs was studied in the presence or absence of quercetin. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence lifetime of NCPDs barely changed in the presence and absence of Qc, indicating the occurrence of static quenching. Therefore, the dynamic quenching can be ruled out, because it is often accompanied by the change of luminophore fluorescence lifetime in the presence or absence of the quencher. Typical UV–vis absorption spectra of Qc, NCPDs and NCPDs-Qc system were displayed in Fig. 5B. It was found that the NCPDs showed a strong absorption peak at 364 nm, while the addition of Qc induced a red-shift, which indicated the interaction between NCPDs and Qc. In ‘hard and soft acids and bases theory’ (HSAB), the phenolic hydroxyl groups (Ar–OH) from Qc belong to soft acids, the CN and amino groups on the surface of NCPDs belong to soft bases (Wu & Chen, 2014), indicating that there is a strong interaction between Qc and NCPDs to form conjugation complex. These complexes may inhibit the transfer of non-radiative electron and hole, and reduce the release of recombinant fluorescence (Zou et al., 2015).

Fig. 5C showed the absorption spectra of Qc and the excitation/emission spectra of NCPDs. However, the emission wavelength of NCPDs didn’t overlap with the absorption of Qc, thus ruling out the FRET process. In contrast, the absorption spectra of Qc and the excitation spectra of NCPDs overlapped partly, which may lead to IFE.
From the above, the quenching mechanism of NCPDs-Qc system mainly derived from the synergistic effect of static quenching and inner filter effect.

To verify the selectivity of the NCPDs-Qc system, aconitine, hypaconitine, mesaconitine, ginsenoside Rb1, ginsenoside Rg1, ginsenoside Rd, notoginsenoside R1, notoginsenoside R2, glucose and urea were selected as interfering substances. As shown in Fig. S2, these potential interferers had little effect on the fluorescence of NCPDs. Therefore, the influence of interference was negligible, indicating the good selectivity of NCPDs towards Qc.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recovery tests in red wine samples
ce:section.ce:para: Standard addition method is one of the methods to evaluate the accuracy of analysis results. In this recovery experiment, a known concentration of Qc was added to red wine and then analyzed. As shown in Table S2, the Qc recoveries of different brands of red wine were within acceptable range of 90% ~ 104.7%, indicating the accuracy of the proposed method. The results further confirmed the feasibility and applicability of this probe for the detection of Qc in real samples.

In this work, a novel and sensitive Qc detection method based on NCPDs was constructed with a dual-mode optical signal output. Based on the synergistic effect of static quenching and inner filter effect, NCPDs could detect Qc with high sensitivity. Moreover, by adding Qc to the solution of probe NCPDs, the visible color changed from colorless to yellow, and the maximum absorption peak in the UV–visible spectrum was red-shifted. The NCPDs could be used as Qc visual detection probe and fluorescence sensor. The wide linear range of fluorescence response and colorimetric method for Qc detection made it more competitive and universal than other Qc probes with single signal readout. Qc detection’s dual-mode strategy might provide a new, efficient, sensitive and immediate visual sensing platform.

Yu Fan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing - review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Jie Yao: Investigation, Data curation. Mengke Huang: Investigation. Chenxi Linghu: Investigation. Jinlin Guo: Visualization, Supervision, Resources. Yang Li: Methodology, Data curation, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the NNSF of China (Grant no. 81603242), Xinglin Scholar Research Premotion Project of Chengdu University of TCM (Grant no. ZRYY1919).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2021.129962.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Alabri et al., 2018

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence spectroscopy-partial least square regression method for the quantification of quercetin in Euphorbia masirahensis
